NICOLE HOLOFCENER AND JEFF WHIT T Y BASED ON THE BOOK BY LEE ISRAEL
S C R E E N P L AY BY
NICOLE HOLOFCENER AND JEFF WHIT T Y
Nicole Holofcener and Jeff Whitty
Based on the book "CAN YOU EVER FORGIVE ME?" By Lee Israel
Final Shooting Script - March 13, 2017
FOX SEARCHLIGHT PICTURES, INC. 10201 W. Pico Blvd.
Los Angeles, CA 90035

ALL RIGHTS RESERVED. COPYRIGHT (c)2016 WILLOW AND OAK, INC. DISPOSAL OF THIS SCRIPT COPY DOES NOT ALTER ANY OF THE RESTRICTIONS SET FORTH ABOVE. Final Shooting Draft 3/13/17
NEW YORK CITY 1991 INT. NEW YORK CITY PRE-WAR APARTMENT - NIGHT Mostly darkness in the small apartment, a few table lamps

illuminating a comfortable, crowded well worn space. On one shelf, souvenirs from a past life: a framed NY Times Best Seller list from 1980 with Kilgallen by Lee Israel highlighted in the number 15 spot. Five or six copies of this book, and a similar number of Miss Tallulah Bankhead (also by Lee) and many more of Estee Lauder by Lee Israel line a shelf. It's a bit of a trophy case. Across the room, a well worn cat tree sits in the corner, piles of New Yorkers, and LEE ISREAL, around 50 and not trying very hard, is asleep in her clothes on the couch. A Blossom Dearie record spins on the record player, a cat snoozes in her lap. Both snore. An alarm beeps on Lee's casio digital watch. Finally, she rouses. She looks down at her watch which reads 10pm, and stops the alarm. She moves to the kitchen, responding slightly to the music. We imagine she may be getting ready for bed. She puts her a plate in the sink, and returns. She downs her water from the table and puts the glass into her purse. She takes a bottle of scotch and also puts it into her purse. She turns off the light, but then the front door opens, hall-light pouring in, and we see Lee exit. The door slams shut.
INT. HIGH-RISE OFFICE BUILDING - MIDDLE OF THE NIGHT

Nondescript cubicles and the nondescript people who are working in them. It is the graveyard shift and the YOUNG EMPLOYEES are proofreading and black-lining law documents. The large room is eerily silent save for the scratching of pencils on paper and the sound of ICE CLINKING in a glass. Lee is working as well, drinking a scotch on the rocks. She swirls it around absentmindedly as she works without enthusiasm. TWO YOUNG WOMEN pass by her cubicle.
(whispering) Fucking kill me if I'm doing this at her age.
Lee hears, and waits til she can't see them.
I'll kill you now if you ask nicely.
Embarrassed giggles. Swirling her glass of scotch, Lee works. Someone shouts from another cubicle, head barely visible
You know we're not allowed to eat or drink in here! (after a beat) Yeah.
Lee ignores them. The first cubical worker tries to peer over to Lee.
A THIRD WORKER (O.S.)
Some laughter. Lee goes back to her work, tries to concentrate. She can't.
(beat) Why do you give a crap? Because the sound of the ice is driving me insane.
Me too! It's making me crazy!
Oh, fuck off.
She drinks, making as much ice noise as possible.
What did you say?
I believe I said fuck off.
A tall MAN, 30, appears at her cubicle.
I'm your boss. Pack up.
I'm almost through with this document. Now.
TALL MAN
He takes the document and other papers from her desk and gestures for her to get up.
(quietly) Please, I need this job.

He ignores her, watching as she puts her things into her canvas bag (including her glass and bottle of scotch). He walks behind her, ushering her out. The young people in the cubicles watch with amusement as she goes. Breaking into loose applause.
INT. CORNER MARKET - VERY EARLY MORNING

Straight from the job, Lee puts her groceries up on the counter - cereal, peanut butter, a can of cat food. On the small black and white TV a program with garishly wealthy characters is playing. The CASHIER, 60s Asian woman, is skeptical.
(she knows her) How much have you got?
(fishing in her pocket) Enough.
Show me first.
Lee counts out her disappointing amount of cash.
Okay, just this.
She separates the cereal and cat food and pays for them.
Put this other stuff back!
Lee walks out.
INT. LEE'S UPPER WEST SIDE APARTMENT - MORNING
Lee opens the cat food and places it in front of her cat JERSEY, who's not interested.
Since when did you become picky.

She puts the cereal on the counter and walks through her cluttered apartment. Peeling paint, way too much stuff and years of stacked up New Yorker magazines. The small one bedroom apartment hasn't been updated in at least 20 years: paint is chipping, furniture is worn and scratched by multiple cats, even the light bulbs need dusting. But you can tell it was once a well-loved little box of a home. The dining table is covered with papers and coffee cups and empty wine bottles, leaving little room for the manual typewriter and old computer.
INT. LEE'S BEDROOM - CONTINUOUS
A few dead flies lie on her pillow. Disgusted, she brushes them off and curls up to sleep in her clothes.
INT. LEE'S BATHROOM - EARLY EVENING
Broken shower tiles, an old moldy curtain. In the medicine cabinet mirror, Lee smooths down her hair and applies lipstick. She notices the litter box in the corner is in unused, pristine condition.
EXT. NEW YORK CITY STREET - EARLY EVENING
Lee approaches the 1 train entrance at 86th street and descends the stairs.
EXT. APT BUILDING - EARLY EVENING
Shivering from the cold, she treks down the New York street.
INT. LARGE APARTMENT - EVENING

Lee has entered a noisy, stylish party. Forty or so festive members of the New York literary world cheerfully enjoy cocktails and conversation. She notices a COAT GUY taking jackets, but she walks past him in search of the bar. Her host MARJORIE, 70 years old and sophisticated, sees her.
Well, this is a surprise.
You invited me, did you not?
You didn't reply.
(ignoring that) A coat check? At my party we threw coats on a bed.
Nice to see you too, Lee.
(beat) You never call me back. I've got some new ideas.
This is why you're here?
You don't know what I've been doing just to get by.
Well, you're here now. Try to enjoy yourself, Lee.

Marjorie excuses herself. The bar as Lee gratefully finds it. She gets her drink and turns, walking through the crowd. She overhears a conversation between TOM CLANCY, 40 famous writer, and a crowd of eager followers.
I don't subscribe to the notion of writers block.
You never experience it?
Writers block is a term invented by the writing community to justify their laziness.
The crowd laughs.
My success is nothing more than that I have the dedication and stamina to sit and get the work done. It's simple.
Having Ronald Reagan talking about you doesn't hurt. Well sure.
Lee passes the conversation, unimpressed.
(walking by) Jackass.
Who's that?
Tom Clancy shrugs, and Lee wanders, not knowing anybody. People hustle by her, having a good time. SEVERAL YOUNG GUESTS are huddled together and Lee eavesdrops.
You have to read them. As an American.
Well, sure.
Lee looks around, landing on SEVERAL OLDER PEOPLE sitting on couches, not talking to anybody. They line the perimeter of the room, sipping their drinks. They're old wallflowers.
INT. HALLWAY - EVENING

Lee walks down the hall that's decorated with framed photographs, book jackets, signed letters - it's Marjorie's professional Memory Lane. She glances up to assess TWO FRAMED BOOK JACKETS. One is KILLGALLEN, by Lee Israel and the other is MISS TALLULAH BANKHEAD also by Lee. Unhappy with their placement, she quickly swaps them out with a couple of frames that are closer to her eye line.
INT. MARJORIE'S POWDER ROOM - EVENING

Lee is snooping in the cabinet and finds a drawer filled with almost finished toilet paper rolls. A dozen of them at least. Weird. She grabs the fullest one and drops it into her large bag. She takes the bar of guest soap as well.
INT. MARJORIE'S APARTMENT - CONTINUOUS
She snakes her way through the party and makes it to the front door just as Marjorie stops her.
That was quick. Did you get properly fed?
This week. Will you take my call?
(condescendingly) Sure. We'll see.
I have fascinating projects! I'm working on a lot of ideas - I just need my agent to call me back.
(laughing to herself) Well, nobody needs a new biography on Fanny Brice, Lee. We may disagree on what is considered fascinating.
She starts to walk away.
Good night Lee.
(stopping her) Why do you have mostly used toilet paper rolls in the drawer of your bathroom? What?
I just can't figure it out.
You went through my bathroom cabinets?
Just tell me. I'm curious.
So that guests can have a full roll.
That's batshit, Marjorie.
(to herself) You would know...
At the coat check Lee points to a very sophisticated, long wool coat.
Yeah, I lost the ticket, but that's the one.
The coat check guy doesn't flinch, but hands it to her.
Have a good night, ma'am.
Lee takes the beautiful coat with a smirk.
INT. LEE'S APARTMENT - LATER THAT NIGHT

Lee sits on her couch, drinking scotch, with her new coat slung over the couch. On the TV Lillian Hellman's black and white film THE LITTLE FOXES plays. It's a scene Lee knows by heart, but that doesn't stop her from enjoying every frame. Bette Davis is snuffing candles whilst quibbling with her brothers Charles Dingle and Carl Benton Reid. Lee loves this.

(along with the TV) There are lots of things to consider. After all, they are first cousins. (then answering herself) Well that isn't unusual. Our grandmother and grandfather were first cousins. (back again) Yes and look at us!
She removes some appetizers from her purse, shoves one into her mouth and offers one to the cat.
Jersey, here girl!
The cat wanders over but doesn't eat them. Lee is concerned.
INT. VETERINARIAN'S OFFICE - DAY
Lee is at the counter, holding a cat carrier and talking to the assistant RACHEL.
You still have a balance, Ms. Israel.
But there's something wrong with her. She's very old.
I'm sorry. The doctor told me to get at least half the balance before we do any tests.
Don't you take a Hippocratic oath or something? Don't you have to do something?
You owe us eighty two dollars.
Lee pulls some bills out of her back pocket.
Look, I've got - fourteen dollars. I'm sorry.
Final Shooting Draft 3/13/17
Lee walks out of the office.
Unbelievable.
EXT. CROSBY STREET BOOKSELLERS - DAY
Lee enters, wearing her stolen coat, carrying two heavy bags of books.
INT. CROSBY STREET BOOKSELLERS - DAY
GLEN, a 30 year old nerd and the used book buyer, glances briefly at Lee's pile of old books on the counter. There are CUSTOMERS waiting behind her.
Just these. That's it? here.
I schlepped them all the way
(dismissive) I'll give you two dollars. I don't want the others.
He goes to the cash register to pay her.
Come on, man. I don't need much. I just don't want to carry them around. (beat) I've got a sick cat and I need the money. Please.
(rude) I told you I don't want the others. There's people waiting.
You don't have to be so disrespectful. You've actually carried my books here.
Glen turns to her, amused.
And you are?
(irritated) I'm Lee Israel.
Oh, Ms. Israel. I do recognize you now. In fact, your latest work is right over there!
He points to a table of books with a sign "75 percent off." Displayed is several copies of Estee Lauder: Behind the Magic.
You rude little ape. What have you ever written?
She turns to leave.
Hey, get your garbage off my counter!
He pushes her books away from him and some fall onto the floor. She scoops them up protectively.
INT. LEE'S APARTMENT BUILDING - ENTRYWAY - DAY
Lee enters her building, and upon seeing Andrei, 50s Romanian, tries to turn around and go right back out, but he calls out.
MS. ISRAEL!
Hi, Andrei.
I knock on your door but you never answer. We're going on three months.
No, I know. I'm really sorry about that. I'm working on it.
I don't want to have to call the management.
Can you cut me a little slack? It's been a rough patch. I just lost my job. I think it was ageism.
Can't you write another book?
Last one was a bit of a calamity.
I like you, you've been here longer than I have, and you've always been kind to my mother. Just get it to me soon.
I will. Absolutely. Thank you.
Andrei start to walk away.
But hey, in the meantime, do you think you could get an exterminator in my apartment? I've got an untenable fly situation.
INT. LEE'S APARTMENT - DAY

Lee drinks coffee and reads the newspaper. She glances briefly at a framed letter on her wall. Reluctantly, she goes to it, takes it from the wall, examining it. It's a framed letter from Kathryn Hepburn. Lee sighs. She knows what she has to do, but she doesn't like it. She takes the letter off the wall.
EXT. TWELVETREES BOOKSELLER - DAY
Lee walks down the street and enters a beautiful shop with First Edition Books and Framed Prints in the window.
INT. TWELVETREES BOOKSELLER - DAY
Inside the dark and cozy store, Lee stands before ANNA, 40, as she reads the Katherine Hepburn letter.

(reading) Lee, Today was to have been the Esquire shoot with Spence. Difficult days, these. Thank you for your sympathy and understanding. Ever Yours, Kate. P.S. Forgive the splotches. All tears lately. (then) Such a lovely apology. (beat) (MORE)
This is very special. Why would you part with it?
(beat) Clutter, you know. I'm not a very sentimental gal.
(appreciatively) Handwritten even. You're the Lee Israel?
Oh, Jesus. Is there another?
I've read your biographies! Have you?
You're a wonderful writer.
Thank you. Can you remind my publisher?
Working on anything new?
Besides my tan?
Anna smiles.
So, how did this letter come about?
I wrote for some magazines a while ago it was going to be a piece on her and Spencer Tracy - but then he died.
Well, her letters usually have the intimacy of a phone book, but this one is really nice. (beat) How does one seventy five sound?
Sounds terrific.
If you ever are feeling sentimental, you can come visit it!
INT. LEE'S APARTMENT - DAY
She takes out a can of cat food and opens it.
I got you the good stuff, you old snob!
She puts it in a bowl on the floor, but the cat doesn't move.
It's the fancy feast.
EXT. JULIUS' BAR - AFTERNOON
Jack enters.
INT. JULIUS' BAR - AFTERNOON
Lee sits drinking, headphones on. Someone takes the seat near her but she doesn't look up. He's about 50, queer and charming. He's JACK HOCK. He gets the attention of CRAIG, the bartender.
Good afternoon, darling. Can I have a beer?
Hi Jack, handsome, I don't know, can you?
Jack quickly pulls out a twenty and puts it on the bar. Craig goes to get the beer. Jack recognizes Lee.
Lee Israel!?
Lee turns and then recoils, never liking to have to talk to anyone. She struggles to pull out a name, taking off her
It's Jack Hock! And the last time I saw you we were both pleasantly pissed at a horrible book party. Am I right?
It's flooding back to me. You were friends with Julia JACK
Steinberg. She's not an agent anymore. She died.
She did? So young.
Or maybe she didn't die. Maybe she just moved to the suburbs - I always confuse those two. No, that's right. She got married and had twins.
Better to have died.
Indeed. (excited) I just came from having my teeth bleached. How do they look?
He smiles wide. His teeth are almost too white.
Why would you do that?
Teeth are a dead give-a-way. Okay.
(beat) Let me buy you a drink. Even though you're the posh writer.
He signals to the bartender.
Well, thank you. I'm trying to remember that party you mentioned. Something keeps flashing in my mind. Something that happened but I can't put my finger on it.
Bad shrimp?
Jack Hock, you said?
That's me. The renegade, the rebel. Jack Hock. Big cock.
He flashes his smile. Lee thinks.
It will come back to me.
I'll try not to be offended. We've been shit faced drunk together more than once, but...
My memory is shot. Don't take it personally.
Craig drops a drink for Lee.
How has life been treating you?
I can actually boast that I've been banned from Crosby Street Booksellers.
What did you do?
I did nothing. People are idiots.
(beat) I'm banned from Duane Reade. (beat) All of them.
She glares at him.
I have a little shoplifting problem.
Lee moves her bag to other side of her stool.
All in the past. But for some reason I have a very memorable mug. (MORE)
Now I have to take a bus just to buy shampoo or aspirin or something. (beat) I'm joking. Duane Reade's not the only rodeo in town.
(dawning on her) You pissed in the closet!
I did what?

I remember now. Nobody could stop talking about the handsome English gentlemanJACK Why thank youLEE Who was so shit-faced he mistook the coat closet for the can. You ruined thousands of dollars worth of furs.
Jack is caught, unsure how this will go over. Well, I-
But Lee loves this story.
Those old ninnies didn't know what had hit them! Their disgusting furs, all covered with piss. Dogs were following them home. I laughed about that for weeks.
Jack starts laughing along with Lee.
Well I'm glad somebody found it amusing. (then) A few folks stopped talking to me after that night. Fuck 'em.
JACK
They cheers.
So, do you work, Jack Hock?
This and that. Mostly that. (beat) I happen to have some very nice coke, if you're interested.
She looks at him, appalled.
Jesus, it's not like I have it all the time. A boy's gotta do what a boy's gotta do. (beat) Anyways, who are you to judge? It's four in the afternoon and you're drunk.
I'm hardly drunk. And it's not "anyways," it's just "anyway."
Well keep drinking then. The day is young.
EXT. JULIUS' BAR - NIGHT
They come out and walk.
INT. SUBWAY - NIGHT
Lee and Jack ride the subway.
You get out the next stop?
(nodding) 79th. You?
I'll get out there. That works. A beat.
I think I saw you in Zabar's once. You were shouting at someone.
Terrible customer service. Been going there for years.
They have really good bread.
They both nod comfortably.
EXT. GLEN'S APARTMENT - NIGHT
A MAN (Glen) comes out of an apartment building with an adorable puppy and attaches it to a leash. Come on, Coco. You're such a good girl. Lee stares at him and taps Jack.
That's the guy - the guy from Crosby Street Booksellers! No!
They watch as he affectionately kisses his dog.
Who knew he was human.
You got a paper and pen?
Lee reaches into her bag and gives him what he needs. He goes into the vestibule of the guy's building and looks to be copying names off the buzzer list.
EXT. UPPER WEST SIDE STREET - NIGHT
They approach Lee's building.
This is me.
Nice place?
It used to be nicer. (beat) And you?
(vague) Oh - a couple blocks up.
She reaches out to shake his hand.
Good-bye Jack. This was not unpleasant.
I'll see you tomorrow? You shall.
She goes into the building.
INT. LEE'S LOBBY - CONTINUOUS
She goes inside and after a moment, turns to see him through the glass door, standing there, looking kind of lost. After a moment, he walks off.
EXT. FANCY TOWNHOUSE - DAY
Lee, dressed neatly, smooths down her hair and knocks on the door. LISA, 20s, answers. She seems put off by Lee's appearance, let's her in. Lisa leads Lee inside.
The employment agency said this was an executive assistant position.
Your name's Lee, right?
Ms. Israel, if you don't mind.
MS. WHITMAN, a wealthy, intimidating woman in her 30s emerges from upstairs.
We do first names here. This is Lisa. I'm Ms. Whitman.
They shake.
INT. MS. WHITMAN'S TOWNHOUSE - DAY
In the enormous living room, Lee is holding a beige colored bra.

Go to Bendels on Fifth. There's two kinds, but both are under wire, 34B. One has lace on the sides and the other is plain. Get them in Apricot. It's new. If you can't find them ask Celeste, she'll help you. Buy four each. Bring that one with you just to make sure.
You know Town shop is just across 86th?
Doesn't matter. And don't take a cab. Take the bus or better yet, take the subway. It's faster.
Lisa enters.
Lisa, fill Lee in on some basics please.
So, what is your job?
That doesn't concern you. She does everything you don't do. Of course.
INT. SUBWAY - DAY

Lee is sitting on the subway with a Bendels bag in her lap. She's reading a book about Fanny Brice. A LITTLE GIRL (7 years old) is standing in front of her, staring at her. Lee tries to ignore her but eventually looks up at the kid. They stare at each other.
Are you a man?
Are you a gargoyle?
The STARTLED MOTHER yanks at her daughter's arm and pulls her away. Lee goes back to her book.
INT. MS. WHITMAN'S APARTMENT - CORRIDOR - SAME DAY
Lisa is showing Lee how to record things in a log book.
So you have to account for how you spend your time each day. If you make a phone call for her, write it here. If you make a phone call for yourself, write it there.
Is this for real?
What do you mean?
Everything I do, I write down? Yes.
Going to the bathroom? Yes.
(beat) You get paid at the end of each day, in cash.
Lisa goes to a drawer in the corridor and takes out two twenty dollar bills.
(off Lee's disappointed face) Ms. Whitman pays what she thinks is fair, considering it's under the table.
EXT. STREET OUTSIDE OF CROSBY STREET BOOKSELLERS - DAY

Across the street, Lee stands in a phone booth as Jack hands her the sheet of paper from the night before, with the list of names from the buzzer. She puts in a quarter and dials. As Lee speaks, they watch the front of the store.

(in thick Long Island accent) Hello, I'm looking for the tall guy, the book buyer, Glen. Oh, hello, thank god! This is Lillian Schuster from the fourth floor. There's an emergency! The building's on fire. We're all outside! Oh my god it's horrible!... no, they're not letting us back in! You better come over and get your dog! I see flames coming out
of your window right now! I think I hear the poor thing squealing! They watch as Glen frantically trips out of the store and runs up the street. They crack up, gratified.
That was very enjoyable.
Thoroughly.
Final Shooting Draft 3/13/17
You do such a good voice!
Lee reaches into her pocket.
I've got no jingle in my jeans. You got a token?
Put out, Jack looks through his change.
INT. VETERINARIAN'S OFFICE - WAITING ROOM - DAY
Lee sits with other PET OWNERS in the waiting room. A YOUNG GIRL is with her DOG.
(to a Lady with cat carrier) She's so cute!
WELL-DRESSED LADY
Oh, I guess that's not good.
Cats. Not for me.
Lee leans over and pets the Old Man's OLD DOG.
He's a good boy, isn't he? The best.
OLD MAN WELL-DRESSED LADY He looks sick.
Just needs a shot. He could use a bath but he doesn't like it.
WELL-DRESSED LADY Pearl here isn't going to like getting declawed but it's going to happen anyway.
It's cruel to de-claw your cat. Excuse me?
WELL-DRESSED LADY
It's like walking on glass for the rest of their lives.
I haven't heard that.
WELL-DRESSED LADY
I would, except it's an object.
The receptionist, Rachel, calls out.
Jersey Israel?
Lee gets up and goes to the counter.
Hi, Ms. Israel. Are you're able to pay for the tests?
That's why I'm here. (beat) You shouldn't de-claw.
Oh, it's harmless. Really.
Lee takes a deep breath.
INT. MS. WHITMAN'S BEDROOM - ANOTHER DAY
Lee is writing a letter by hand for Ms. Whitman while she dictates from her bed.
The event was clearly a success and I couldn't have succeeded without you. Your help and advice have been very generous
Are you sure you want to use success two times? Excuse me?
MS. WHITMAN
(reading) 'Clearly a success and succeeded.' MS. WHITMAN I'm sorry, did I ask you for advice?
I just thought MS. WHITMAN I have a Masters degree in English, Lee.
And I'm a published author, Ms. Whitman.
Whitman sighs deeply, thinking she's being mocked.
Can we continue, please? Of course.
Now I lost my train of thought.
INT. MS. WHITMAN'S LIBRARY - LATER THAT DAY
Lee is at the large bookshelf organizing and dusting the books. Surprised, she finds her own book MISS TALLULAH BANKHEAD, and pulls it out. Lisa enters the room.
Ms. Whitman doesn't like anyone touching the books.
How can I possibly organize and clean them if I cannot touch them?
I mean, you know what I mean.
Ms. Whitman enters. MS. WHITMAN Lee, I need you to balance Natalie's
Your teenager.
Yes, I've opened an account for her. She needs to learn responsibility.
By me balancing her checkbook?
Ms. Whitman looks at her, coldly considering her.
If you want to borrow a book, simply ask before handling it.
I mentioned that I was a published author. This is my book.
Ms. Whitman inhales.
I get the sense you're not very happy here.
I'm giddy.
You're sarcastic and impolite. (beat, leaving) You'll be paid for your time here.
If you can call it pay.
EXT. FANCY TOWNHOUSE - DAY
Lisa hands Lee two twenties and then slams the door.
INT. FANCY TOWNHOUSE - DAY
Ms. Whitman is holding the Tallulah Bankhead book, looking with shock at Lee's photo on the back.
INT. MS. WHITMAN'S APARTMENT - CORRIDOR - CONTINUOUS
Lisa silently reads Lee's log, mouth agape. 1:45 - 1:48. Took a dump and scratched
3:22 - 3:26. Got fired. 3:27-3:28 Took another dump. Forgot to flush. Oops.
INT. JULIUS' BAR - AFTERNOON
Lee enters the bar, looks around, and sees Jack a few stools away. She sits away from him.
I rang you and you didn't ring me back. Should I take it personally?
Why wouldn't you?
I'd never get out of bed.
And lucky for me, you did.
She's somewhat entertained by him, in spite of herself.
I'm coming to sit next to you because I'm not very good at social cues.
He moves next to her and Lee speaks to Craig, the bartender.
Scotch, water, Craig. And I'll buy his next one.
You got it.
Oh, things are looking up!
Got shit-canned again.
That is exactly the reason I don't get a job. (beat) You're a successful writer. Aren't you writing a book?
On Fanny Brice. Who?
Just the greatest vaudeville comedian of her time. You sure you're a fag?
EXT. LINCOLN CENTER LIBRARY - DAY
Lee crosses into the large library.
INT. LINCOLN CENTER LIBRARY - DAY

The table is covered with articles and books. Lee is taking notes on a pad. She separates some papers and finds TWO TYPED LETTERS SIGNED BY FANNY BRICE. Her eyes widen. She looks around - to let someone know the letters don't belong here - and then she changes her mind. She reads them with a kind of secret thrill. She glances around the room, relieved that no one is looking her way. Quickly, she drops the letters into her canvas bag.
INT. TWELVETREES BOOKSELLER - DAY
Anna greets Lee as she enters the shop.
Oh, hi Lee. Nice to see you again.
You as well.
How've you been?
Well, I've discovered that my cousin Sydney was in possession of this delightful bonne bouche. I thought you might be interested in it.
She hands her one of the Fanny Brice letters, protected in plastic.
Fanny Brice. One of my favorites.
(finally!) I've been working on a new biography on her.
What a funny coincidence then, that your cousin had this! (beat) Well, she's much more your style than Estee Lauder.
Right? Thank you.
Didn't she say "Wrong is for other people."? I love that.
(impressed) She did.
Anna smiles and looks to the letter.
Well, what do you think?
I believe I could sell this. (beat) I could offer you seventy five.
(disappointed)
I could pay more for better content. It's a bit bland, is all.
(glancing at the letter) True.
Anna goes to write a check.
Would you be able to pay cash?

Let me see if I have enough. (she checks a drawer) I'm glad to hear you're writing another book! I'll be the first to buy it. Did you hear Tom Clancy is going to get paid 3 million dollars to write more rightwing macho bullshit? What is the state of the literary world?
Are you kidding me? That blowhard is getting paid three million dollars?
Anna is taken aback by Lee's reaction.

(to Lee) Lee I'm so sorry. It probably just happened. It's not my fault.
I gave her the pills. I did what you told me, Lee. (beat) Let me in!
She pulls the dead cat from under the couch. Kneeling by the couch, she cradles it in her lap as she cries.
(under her breath) I'm sorry, Jersey. I shouldn't have left you.
EXT. VETERINARIAN'S OFFICE - DAY
Lee holds a shoe box and walks into the vet's building.
INT. VETERINARIAN'S OFFICE - DAY
She walks up to Rachel at the counter.
Hi, Ms. Israel. Is Jersey okay?
Lee can only manage to shake her head.
Oh. I'm so sorry.
I don't have a backyard or anything. (beat) I didn't know what to do with her.
Rachel now sees the box.
Oh, we can take care of that.
Lee hands it to her.
I'm sorry for your loss.
Lee heads for the door.
INT. LEE'S APARTMENT - NIGHT
Lee is in the kitchen, drunk and pacing and dialing the phone.
MARIE'S VOICE
Yes, I'd like to speak to Elaine.
MARIE'S VOICE Is this Lee?
Is she there please?
Actually she's LEE (cutting her off) I just had something of importance to tell her. At what time do you expect her return?
I'm not actually sure. (beat) You've got to stop calling here.
I don't have to do anything, Marie. I just need people to take my calls, instead of having some piece of shit tell me what I have to do. That's what I think I deserve.
She slams the phone down.
INT. JULIUS' BAR - DAY
At a table, Lee hands Jack a bunch of letters in a folder. She is short, averting her eyes. She is all business, cold.
Let them authenticate all they want. They're real. Don't settle.
Lee - are we going to talk about it? I am really sorry.
She stays the course.
Did you make an appointment at Armada?
Yes. I rang him. He said to come at one.
All right. Afterward, come right here.
I'll do good, you'll see. I'll make it up to you.
Lee doesn't reply to this. There is no making it up.
Get a written receipt. I want to know how much you're paid.
Okay, if that's what you want. He'll give it to me. That little fucker eats up my
(laughing a little) Ooh that sounded dirty.
He laughs, trying to engage Lee like they used to. She won't.
Come on. How long are you going to freeze me out?
(beat) I trusted you. I don't know if you've noticed, but I don't do that. You have reminded me why that is.
(sincerely pleading) Lee, I took care of the cat. I swear. It was an accident, and I feel awful.
And your little friend? Who was not supposed to be in my house. Does he feel awful too?
I don't know. I haven't seen him.
We will continue doing business together because I have no options, but we aren't friends, I don't think we actually ever were.
We will meet in public. You will not fuck this up like you fuck everything else up in your life. Sell the letters. Get the money. And get a fucking receipt.
Fine. (after a moment, one more try) I onlyI'm done.
She looks him in the eye for the first time. He understands that it's over.
I'll be back after the sale.
He gets up slowly and walks away. Lee sits stoically.
EXT. ARMADA BOOKSELLERS - DAY
Jack walks up the street, smoking, nervous, headed for Armada Booksellers.
INT. ARMADA BOOKSELLERS - SOON AFTER
Paul, more uneasy than usual, is handling the letters that Jack has offered him.
Aren't these fabulous? I just know someone is going to treasure them.
They're terrific. Where on earth did you find these?
My granny just passed and I discovered them when I was cleaning out her junk. They're worth something, right?
How much are you asking for them?
I guess around three hundred each?
Paul goes to write a check. Jack is taken aback by how easy
Actually, do you have cash?
Paul instantly counts out six hundred dollars cash and puts it in Jack's hand.
INT. JULIUS' BAR - DAY
Already hammered, Lee sits at the bar, scribbling in a notebook, doing calculations of the money she owes. She is agitated and more than a little drunk.
(to the bartender) I need another.
Craig comes over, starts to pour.
That's debateable.
(too defensive) What did you say?
Craig looks up, surprised by her anger.
Nothing, Lee.
He walks away, shaking his head a little.
(to herself) Fuckin' judgemental jack-ass.
INT. ARMADA BOOKSELLERS - DAY
Jack is cornered by Solanas and Doyle.
(in a panic) I'm pretty sure they're stolen, I didn't want to ask. But she asked me to sell them as a favor so I said okay!
As a favor? You don't accept a cut?
Well, a small one. Very small. But I don't know where they came from, honest! I'll tell you anything you want to know. Am I in big trouble?
He gives his puppy dog eyes.
INT. JULIUS' BAR - CONTINUOUS

Lee is drunk, looks up at a picture of Tallulah Bankhead on the wall, amongst all the other famous people who used to frequent the bar. Then back to her drink. Empties it. She is mumbling to herself. This is a Lee we haven't seen before more incoherent, nervous. The anxiety is getting to her.
(mumbling, barely audible) ...probably ran off with the money. I'll kill him....
(from end of the bar) Did you say something?
Lee shakes her head, shooing him off. She crumples the piece of paper she was scribbling on. Looks back to the picture, weirdly distracted by it. Glances at the clock. It's 2:30.
(mumbling, only making out every couple of words) No, at least until 3... (then, calling to Craig) Actually one more.
Craig sets one down next to her.
(pointing to the picture) I wrote a biography of her once, in another life.
Yes, you've told me.
More and more festive people are crowding into the bar. Craig attends to the other patrons. Someone tries to sit at the stool next to Lee.
No! I'm waiting for someone.
The guy rolls his eyes, walking off. Lee puts on her headphones, to drown out the crowd.
(muttering to herself) Nobody has any patience anymore.
People are crowding into Lee's space though. Finally, Lee gets up to move down the bar, comes face to face with the FBI agents, approaching her.
Lee Israel?
She takes off her headphones, confused. The patrons of the bar, as well as Craig all notice what's going on. It takes Lee a moment.
I'm agent Solanas and this is Agent Doyle. We're here to deliver a subpoena to appear before a federal grand jury.
He hands Lee an envelope.
Your appearance is scheduled for two weeks from today. You will find the address on the enclosed documents. You are forbidden to destroy any evidence pertinent to this case.
I don't know what you're talking about.
We've intercepted your employee, Jack Hock. He is cooperating as are the dealers you did business with. (beat) Anything you'd like to say? Anything on your mind that you'd like to get off your chest?
I think it behooves me to be a wallflower at this particular dance. Until I can get
a hold of an attorney.
As you wish. (beat) Mr. Hock requests that you not try to reach him or telephone or harass him in any way. And by law you are forbidden to contact him regardless.
Lee is stung.
INT. LEE'S APARTMENT - DAY
Frantic, Lee attacks her research with a scissors, throwing the pieces into different garbage bags. She rips papers and crumples up anything she can find.
EXT. UPPER WEST SIDE STREET - DAY

Carrying several black plastic bags, Lee hustles down the street. She glances around her, paranoid, chucking bags into various piles of street garbage waiting for pick up. One by one, Lee throws a typewriter into a different garbage can.
INT. LAW OFFICE - DAY
Lee sits opposite an UPTIGHT FEMALE LAWYER. She's picking cat hair off her old jacket, visibly agitated. UPTIGHT LAWYER
I could've told you that. But you have a very good reputation, surely you can -

(cuts her off) Ms. Israel, I'm afraid that you will spend a fair amount of time behind bars, despite my reputation. (beat) You were deliberate, your crimes were premeditated and I have to say, numerous. It says here (reads) You created and sold approximately four hundred letters. That many?
You're proud.
Did you read them?
No. Why would I read them? (considering) I believe the only way to handle this is to plead insanity and frankly, I don't think we'd have any problem proving it.
Lee stares at her, doesn't like this one bit.
INT. LAW OFFICE - DAY
Lee sits across from LLOYD, a handsome lawyer. She rubs her temples.
Awful.
LEE
Can't blame you. (reading papers) Looking at all this... it's pretty bad. Now that said, can I say - those letters were pretty damned impressive. I especially enjoyed the Louise Brooks.
Thank you. (beat) I'm afraid they were my finest moment.

I'm sure that's not true. (beat) You fell on hard times. You had a sick cat. You couldn't get a writing job and you didn't mean for it to get out of hand, but then some jerk blackmails you and you panic. You never meant for it to continue, or to steal, but you had to pay him back or you'd go to jail. (beat) Depending on the judge, I think at worst you could conceivably get a year and change.
Lee looks panicked.
You'll bring a book. (beat) Look, if you want me to represent you, you're going to have to do a few things before appearing in court. Like what?
Clean up your act. First, you're getting a job. Any kind of job. And second, you're enrolling in community service to show how penitent you are.
What - what kind of service?
Something with children? Jesus no.
Animals, sick people. Whatever. You're going to show that you have turned over a new leaf. (BEAT) You've got to go to AA.
That's absurd.
He stares at her. It doesn't seem possible that her spirit could sink even lower.
I want to ask you something. (beat) So, everyone I sold to - do you think they all know?
Yeah. They probably all know. But right now, that's the least of your worries.
INT. JULIUS' BAR - DAY
In the mostly empty bar, dressed for court, Lee sits and drinks, somewhat a pariah. A few regulars whisper about her.
EXT. COURTHOUSE - DAY
Lee walks up the steps of the intimidating courthouse.
INT. COURTHOUSE - DAY
Lee and Lloyd sit on a bench outside the courtroom. Lee is sweating in her heavy jacket as she anxiously rubs her hands against her thighs.
We're very lucky this is our judge. Very lucky. She doesn't waste time with things like this. I have a good feeling. Try to breathe.
I'm scared.
(handing her a mint, annoyed) You smell like alcohol.
She shrugs and takes the mint.
INT. COURTROOM - DAY
In a simple, nearly empty courtroom, Lee and Lloyd stand before the JUDGE.

(continuing) She is seeking employment with Scholastic books as a copy editor, and has already put in over forty hours of community service at a cat shelter. JUDGE (to Lee) Before I render my verdict, have you in the last twenty-four hours used drugs, pills, alcohol, anything that could affect your ability to understand what is going on? No ma'am.
Have you anything additional you would like to say to the court before I offer your sentence? Yes.
Lloyd watches her carefully as she stands, and takes a deep breath. What comes next begins as something rehearsed, but devolves into a stream-of-consciousness confession.

I have been living in a state of enormous guilt and anxiety for the past year. Not because I felt like I was doing something wrong, but because I was always afraid of being found out. I can't specifically say
that I regret my actionsLloyd clears his throat, urging Lee to watch herself.

I don't. I thoroughly enjoyed writing these letters, living in the world of Dorothy Parker and Noel Coward, pretending I was something I am not. In many ways, this has been the best time of my life. It's the only time recently I can remember being proud of the work I was doing. (then) (MORE)

But it wasn't my work, was it? I was hiding behind these people, their names. Because if I'd actually put myself out there, done my own work, then I would be opening myself up to criticism. And I'm too much of a coward for all of that. I've lost my cat- the only soul that truly loved me, maybe ever- and I lost my friend - who might have been an idiot, but tolerated me, and was nice to have around. And I've realized that I'm not a real writer. In the end, it was not worth it.
This hits Lee in a surprising way. Lloyd looks at her nervously. She returns to her script.
I will accept the judgement of the court as valid and fulfill whatever sentence I may receive with the full understanding that I have earned said punishment.
A moment. The judge nods.

Lee Israel, the court sentences you to five years probation plus six months house arrest. You may only leave home to go to and from your place of employment, your work in the community, and to alcoholic's anonymous meetings.
(blurting) I don't have anywhere to go.
You are not to leave the state or consort with felons and you are to pay restitution to your victims within your means.
Lloyd grins at Lee, nodding. Lee stares, in disbelief.
(quietly to Lee) This is a good thing. Very fair.
But Lee is reeling. The weight of everything is finally settled on her, and it is heavy.
(at her lowest) Mm hmm. Good.
Lloyd doesn't know what to say.
EXT. COURTHOUSE - DAY
Lee walks out of the courthouse emptied and exhausted.
INT. LEE'S APARTMENT - DAY

Lee enters her apartment. It is eerily empty. No typewriters. No Jersey. No Jack. Jersey's empty food bowl sits on the floor, taunting Lee. She checks her answering machine but has no messages. She collapses onto the couch. She does not know where she will go from here.
EXT. CENTRAL PARK - DAY
Lee sits on a bench waiting. After a moment she sees ELAINE, 45, coming toward her.
Thanks for coming.
It sounded important.
It was. It is. My phone got shut off. In case you tried to call. I didn't.
ELAINE
They sit down and Elaine notices the cat hair on Lee's jacket, picks at it.
I see you brought Jersey with you. How is that old bitch?
That's what I wanted to tell you, Elaine. She just died. Oh.
I figured, since she was a gift from you-
Lee starts to tear up.
Was she? (beat) I'm so sorry, Lee.
(shrugging) I got too attached to her.
You going to get another?
No. I can't replace her. It wouldn't be right.
Maybe in time.
I do go to Aunt Eleanor's pretty regularly.
Oh god, I haven't thought about that place in years! It's still there? On
Exactly the same. Different cats.
(beat) You still on 82nd?
Where else would I be?
You look all right. You all right?
You can't imagine what I've had to do to survive.
We all have to do things we don't want to do.
Maybe. I can't remember the last time I was doing something because I wanted to do it.
Elaine examines Lee, concerned.
Are you sleeping, Lee?
This bit of kindness hits Lee.
Fits and starts. But when you can't recognize your own life it's hard to find rest.
You're having one of those times. You just gotta ride it out.
I was supposed to be something so different than this. Wasn't I? When we were together, people liked my work right?
That's why you wanted to see me?
No. I mean... I've wanted to see you for a long time. I've needed to see you, Elaine. I just... I'm in a crisis. And you know me.
It always seemed more important to you that people liked your work than that they liked you.
Well, of course.
No, Lee. That's not healthy.
I guess not. ButELAINE You were so miserable.
No I wasn't.
Yes you were.
A beat.
I was happy with you.
Well, I appreciate that. We were happy there. For parts of it.
It was more than parts of it.
No, Lee. There was always a wall between us. Something I couldn't penetrate.
I tried. God damnit. I tried more with you than with anyone ever.
No you didn't. You did everything you could to keep your distance. You lied, you drank constantly, you were selfinvolvedLEE Well, I didn't say I was perfect.
And the moment anyone wronged you, you cut them out. It was like you were looking for everyone to fail you, so you wouldn't have stay connected.
Well fuck. I always thought of myself as pleasantly prickly.
More like a fortress of disdain. I could never get you to let down the wall- to trust me- and at some point I just stopped trying.
I wish you hadn't.
I don't know why you were so afraid for anyone to get to know you - maybe you thought nobody would like what they saw. Such a shame.
This takes Lee by surprise. Elaine starts to stand up, Lee grabs her arm.
Wait - don't go.
This isn't my job anymore, Lee. To talk you off the ledge. It's exhausting.
Who is she?
Elaine sighs, she was trying to avoid this. Lee.
I'm just curious.
She's a public advocate. She's warm and it's simple.
Is she always a cunt to your old friends? I wouldn't let her answer the phone if I were you.

And there it is. I don't know why I try. (she makes a motion of a wall going up) You're never going to change. Everyone is always wronging you - nobody appreciates you, nobody gives you what you deserve.... (short beat) And nothing is ever your fault.
Lee is stunned.
I've got a class. (then) (MORE)
It was good to see you, for a minute there.
She turns and walks away.
(beat) Say hi to Marie!
Elaine doesn't turn around and Lee sits there, hating herself.
INT. LEE'S APARTMENT - BEDROOM - NIGHT
Lee lies in bed, staring up at the fan. She isn't filled with anxiety anymore. She is numb. She is lifeless, replaying Elaine's words in her head. She pulls out Anna's short story and begins to read.
EXT. TWELVETREES BOOK SHOP - DAY

Lee walks past Anna's bookshop. She sees Anna inside, talking to a customer. She knows she can't go in. She stops and watches though. Suddenly Anna looks up and the two women make eye contact. Anna breaks it and when she looks back up, Lee is gone. Sadly, Lee walks away.
INT. LEE'S APARTMENT - DAY
Lee stares for a while. Finds nothing. She goes and sits at her one remaining, original type-writer. She puts in a piece of paper. She thinks. Final Shooting Draft 3/13/17
INT. JULIUS' BAR - AFTERNOON - SOME MONTHS LATER
Lee sits at a table - she doesn't look good but she doesn't look bad either. Craig brings her a scotch.
Haven't seen your face in here in a while.
Yeah well, I'm only allowed to leave the
house for my AA meetings.

Craig walks away shaking his head. Suddenly the door opens and Jack comes in. He sees Lee, approaches her table. Is this a coincidence? Jack looks a little thin, and walks with a crutch, babying one foot.
Thanks for coming.
(shrugging) It was quite inconvenient. I had to move around some meetings, but I had my assistant cancel my afternoon.
Lee smirks. It's kind of Jack to make this easier.
Can I get you a drink? I'm buying. I've started counterfeiting money now.
She smirks, this tickles Jack.
Not today. (off Lee's surprise) It's my new meds. Throwing my whole system off. Gin tastes like mouthwash. Takes the fun out of it.
Lee is concerned, but doesn't press.
I guess you were able to cut a deal? Ratting on me worked out?
Three years probation.
At least you're out and about.
So are you.
No, I'm at an AA meeting on 10th street.
Jack laughs.
Criminals at large! (then) I couldn't imagine what was so important that Lee Israel swallowed her pride and asked me to meet her. So spit it out.
I'm reconsidering.
Oh just tell me.
Well... I've been thinking about writing a new book. About what happened. About what I did. And about you, if you'll let me.
Like hell I will! What would my boyfriend think if he knew about my shadier dealings?
Do our crimes even count in your top ten of shady dealings? I doubt it.
Fair enough. But I don't want a book out there about me. I'm a very private person.
Lee is quiet.
I have to do something. I have to write again.
What about Fanny Price?
Brice? No. I think for once I would like to write about one Lee Isreal. As terrifying as that sounds.
I'm still mad at you, you know. You treated me like shit. I don't think you're a very nice person.
I would agree with you.
But I suppose you may be mad at me as well.
If you didn't look quite so decrepit, I would be.
(quietly) Yes, well. I guess it's not some huge plot twist. It was going to catch up to me eventually.
You have fucked your way through Manhattan.
I would like that on my tombstone. They share a morbid laugh.
With the addition of: but he steered clear of the boroughs. Sure.
After a beat.
Will you make me 29? And with perfect skin? And don't make me sound stupid?
Lee is quiet, realizing he is agreeing.
Oh fuck it. Write whatever you want. It's not like anyone reads your books anyways.
I've already written five chapters.
Jack laughs.
(quietly) Thank you.
Lee reaches out and touches Jack's hand. This lingers for just a moment and then Jack stands, breaking it.
I'm late for a board meeting. My driver is waiting. The life of a millionaire.
They both smile, going with the bit. Jack starts toward the door. Crossing in front of Lee. Lee stifles a little laugh. Jack looks back... What?
No... I was so tempted to trip you, just now.
Jack laughs, against his better judgement.
Wow, you're ... a horrid cunt, Lee.
So are you, Jack. Good luck. You too.
LEE
He leaves.
INT. LEE'S APARTMENT - DAY
Sitting at her dining table, Lee writes on an old, large computer. She sips scotch. A kitten scampers around her ankle and settles on the keyboard. The place is cleaned up again, but still Lee's.
EXT. CENTRAL PARK - DAY
Springtime. Lee sits on a bench, content. She eats a hot dog, and watches people pass.
EXT. STREET/LAST BOOKSTORE - DAY
Lee walks down the street and backtracks, having seen something in a bookstore window that has caught her eye. It's a mounted letter from Dorothy Parker. One that Lee has written.
INT. RENWICK & CO. RARE BOOKS - DAY
Lee addresses the bookseller.
My friend's birthday is coming up and he'd love to own that Dorothy Parker in
the window.
She's a beauty, isn't she?
Can I ask how much a letter like that runs?
We're asking nineteen hundred, framed and matted, and she comes complete with a letter of authenticity.
Lee's eyes widen.
Does the letter of authenticity come with a letter of authenticity?
Oh, I can assure you. It's the real thing. No one can write like Ms. Parker could. Not before and not since.
The bittersweet compliment is music to her ears.
Well, that might be out of my price range, but thank you very much.
INT. LEE'S APARTMENT - DAY
Lee sits at her old typewriter (the one she already owned), and writes.
EXT. RENWICK & CO. RARE BOOKS - DAY
We see the man through the front window, reading this letter. He looks into the distance, considering. Disturbed, he moves to the shop window.
I dearly hope this letter will not affect the selling price of your valuable artifact. As I most famously muttered mere moments after my cremation:
"Darling, excuse my dust."
